data.title: Pharmaceutical tariffs still in play despite Supreme Court ruling
data.description:
• Ongoing threats from President Trump to enact tariffs on pharmaceutical products imported into the United States could still become reality despite Friday's Supreme Court ruling striking down his authority to impose so-called "reciprocal" tariffs.
  • That's because the pharma tariffs were enacted as part of Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, which is not impacted by the High Court's decision. Section 232 covers products that potentially threaten national security. 
  • Trump claimed authority for the "reciprocal" tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977. The Supreme Court ruled the president does not have that ability.
  • However, many pharmaceutical companies don't have to worry about the tariff threat thanks to deals negotiated with the White House through the "most favored nation" policy. In exchange for a three-year pass on tariffs, these pharmas are providing some of their medicines at a discounted rate through the TrumpRx website and boosting manufacturing in the US.
  • These companies include Pfizer (PFE), Eli Lilly (LLY), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Merck (MRK), Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY), Amgen (AMGN), Gilead Sciences (GILD), GSK (GSK), Novartis (NVS), Sanofi (SNY), and Roche (RHHBY)

data.title: Top Coal and Consumable Fuels Stocks by Year-To-Date Performance
data.description:
Year to date, the energy sector has been one of the market’s strongest performers. In light of this, below is a list of coal and consumable fuels stocks arranged by their year-to-date performance.

The list is topped by Uranium Energy Corp. (UEC), with a YTD performance of 37.93%. American Resources Corporation (AREC) and Natural Resource Partners L.P. (NRP) are next, with Peabody Energy Corporation (BTU) and NACCO Industries, Inc. (NC) rounding out the rest of the top five.

Mid-range performers include Alliance Resource Partners, L.P. (ARLP) and Lightbridge Corporation (LTBR), while Core Natural Resources, Inc. (CNR) shows modest gains. Quant ratings across the sector vary, with most stocks rated as “Hold” while Centrus Energy Corp. (LEU) carries a “Sell” rating and several stocks are not covered.

Here is the list:

data.title: Century Aluminum outlines 630,000-ton shipment target for 2026 as Oklahoma smelter and Mt. Holly expansion advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Century Aluminum Company (CENX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jesse Gary, President and CEO, highlighted Century's leadership in the American aluminum market, referencing "exciting developments on our Oklahoma smelter partnership with EGA and the redevelopment of the Hawesville site into an AI digital infrastructure campus." Gary described the Oklahoma smelter project as "the first new smelter in the U.S. in nearly 50 years," with EGA owning 60% and Century 40%, supported by a $500 million Department of Energy grant and Bechtel retained for engineering. The smelter will use EGA’s EX technology, increasing projected size to 750,000 metric tons, which Gary stated "will more than double total U.S. aluminum production."
  • The recent sale and redevelopment of the Hawesville site brought in $200 million in cash and a 6.8% interest in the completed data center, with Gary noting the potential for "returns well in excess of the initial cash payment."
  • Gary reported "excellent performance across our smelter assets in the fourth quarter," commending Sebree for a "record year for the smelter across a suite of KPIs and profitability metrics."
  • At Jamalco, Gary highlighted hurricane preparedness and the near completion of a new on-site power turbine (TG4), expected to "substantially lower the cost structure of the refinery."
  • Gary announced repairs at Grundartangi enabling restart of Line 2 by end of April, "about 6 months sooner than originally anticipated," and confirmed insurance coverage for the outage.
  • CFO Peter Trpkovski stated, "Net sales for the quarter were $634 million, a $2 million increase sequentially," and noted, "we reported net income of $1.8 million or $0.02 per share. Our adjusted net income was $128 million or $1.25 per share, excluding exceptional items."

Outlook

  • Trpkovski provided Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance of "$215 million to $235 million," attributing improvement to higher realized LME and regional premiums, with expectations for "a $70 million to $80 million increase to Q1 adjusted EBITDA when compared with Q4 levels."
  • For 2026, Century expects to ship "approximately 630,000 tons of primary aluminum," reflecting the impact of the Mt. Holly expansion and earlier-than-expected Grundartangi restart. Once all projects are complete, annualized production would reach "closer to 750,000 tons per year."
  • Century anticipates "total CapEx for the year in the range of $115 million to $125 million" and expects cash interest to decline in 2026 due to lower coupon on senior notes and a simplified capital structure.
  • Gary stated, "2026 is setting up to be a historic year for Century, and we are laser-focused on execution to benefit from the opportunities that are in front of us."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter shipments were "approximately 140,000 tons, a decrease from the prior quarter due to the line loss in Iceland."
  • Trpkovski reported, "Adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter increased $70 million to $171 million."
  • Century ended Q4 with a cash balance of $134 million, reducing net debt to $421 million.
  • The company generated operating cash flow of $170 million in Q4 and received a $75 million 45X tax credit, with a $173 million receivable outstanding for additional credits.
  • The Hawesville site sale proceeds are not included in year-end cash.

Q&A

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley Securities: Asked if Q1 guidance adds back lost EBITDA from Grundartangi. Trpkovski confirmed, "That's correct. Similar to how we did on the last call."
  • Giles questioned earnings power in different price environments and capital allocation. Trpkovski detailed price sensitivities, noting an uplift from current spot prices and that "in revenue and in power combined, that's about a $75 million uplift from our midpoint if you're taking the guide of $225 million to spot."
  • Giles inquired about the Oklahoma smelter's power contract. Gary responded, "We are working on finalizing that power contract with EGA and with PSO, who is the power provider... for an investment of this size, that power contract needs to be enabling and attractive."
  • Katja Jancic, BMO: Asked about next milestones for the new smelter. Gary listed finalizing the power contract, completing engineering with Bechtel, and progressing on financing, aiming for a final investment decision in Q4 2026.
  • Jancic asked about government project-level financing; Gary indicated, "There are a number of financing options available... some of which are potentially available from the government."
  • Matthew Key, Texas Capital: Asked about Iceland outage shipment cadence. Gary explained, "Line 1... is producing about 1/3 of the overall Grundartangi volume" until Line 2 is back up by the end of July, with full utilization expected by August.
  • Key asked if the Hawesville data center stake would fund the new smelter. Gary replied, "We will be generating significant EBITDA and cash flow just from the regular operation of the business that should be more than sufficient to cover any financing needs."
  • Giles in a follow-up asked about uses of excess cash. Gary stated, "We always have the capability to pay down debt... and then if we do have cash left over, we will look at returns to shareholders."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on operational execution, capital allocation, and project timelines, with appreciation shown for strategic moves like the Hawesville sale.
  • Management’s tone was confident in both prepared remarks and Q&A, expressing certainty about market opportunities and operational progress, with repeated emphasis on execution and capital returns. Gary asserted, "We are confident that this equity stake should provide returns well in excess of the initial cash payment."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence increased, notably due to accelerated project timelines and successful asset sales. Analysts continued to probe on capital allocation and project execution but showed greater confidence in management’s guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q1 adjusted EBITDA increased versus the prior quarter, with Q4 guidance at $170 million to $180 million and Q1 now projected at $215 million to $235 million.
  • The Oklahoma smelter project advanced from discussions with potential partners and power providers to a formal partnership with EGA and Bechtel engaged for engineering.
  • The Hawesville site review concluded with a sale and future equity stake, compared to extended review and uncertainty in Q3.
  • Restart timeline for Grundartangi accelerated by six months, versus prior uncertainty pending transformer repairs.
  • Management tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to heightened confidence and execution focus in Q4, reinforced by successful project milestones and a favorable market environment.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from risk and timeline concerns to clarifications on sensitivities, capital allocation, and long-term strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing supply chain constraints for transformers at Grundartangi, but confirmed insurance coverage and earlier-than-anticipated repairs.
  • Instability in Jamaica’s power grid post-hurricane led to higher costs and production outages at Jamalco, though the new on-site turbine is expected to mitigate future risks.
  • Temporary U.S. energy price spikes impacted Sebree, although management expects normalization in future quarters.
  • Analysts raised questions about power contracts for Oklahoma and the ability to achieve favorable terms, as well as the timing and value realization from the Hawesville data center stake.

Final Takeaway

Century Aluminum’s Q4 2025 call underscored a pivotal period of transformation and accelerated execution, with major milestones including a formalized Oklahoma smelter joint venture, the Hawesville site monetization, and a six-month acceleration of the Grundartangi restart. Management projects significant volume growth and improved profitability in 2026, supported by favorable aluminum market conditions and strong capital discipline, while maintaining focus on risk mitigation and value creation for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Catalyst Watch: Nvidia blockbuster, JPMorgan event, and reactions to the tariff reset
data.description:
Welcome to Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch - a breakdown of some of next week's actionable events that stand out. Read more about the events that may impact stock prices next week.

 

Monday - February 23

  • Tariff watch - Analysts and investors will be busy resetting expectations on nearly every sector after the Supreme Court ruling on President Trump's sweeping tariff policies.
  • Volatility watch - AXT (AXTI) and CoreWeave (CRWV) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Sensei Bio (SNSE), Calumet (CLMT), and Energy Services of America (ESOA). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Virtuix (VTIX), SPS Commerce (SPSC), and SimilarWeb (SMWB).
  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Dominion Energy (D), Diamondback Energy (FANG), and Domino's Pizza (DPZ). Options trading implies a double-digit swing in share price for Bed Bath & Beyond (BBBY) and Vir Biotechnology (VIR) after they report.
  • Dividend watch - Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Marriott International (MAR), Delta Air Lines (DAL), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and Northrop Grumman (NOC).
  • IPO watch - The quiet period ends on York Space Systems (YSS), PicS (PICS), Ethos Technologies (LIFE), and Public Policy Holding (PPHC) to free up analysts to post ratings. The IPO lockup period expires on blocks of shares of Picard Medical (PMI).
  • All day - Fed Governor Lisa Cook will participate in a panel on “Artificial Intelligence and Productivity” at the NABE Annual Economic Policy Conference in Washington, D.C.
  • 8:25 a.m. JPMorgan (JPM) will host a company update in New York City. Analysts expect the firm-wide overview by CFO Jeremy Barnum to be more in-depth than in recent years, as it will likely include key points for each main business line. CEO Jamie Dimon is anticipated to close out the event with prepared remarks and Q&A.

 

Tuesday - February 24

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Home Depot (HD), MercadoLibre (MELI), American Tower (AMT), Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP), and Workday (WDAY). Options trading implies a double-digit swing in share price for AMC Entertainment (AMC) and Revolve Group (RVLV) after they report.
  • All day - Apple (AAPL) will hold its annual meeting. Shareholders will vote on a proposal from the National Center for Public Policy Research requesting a board-level report on risks and costs of Apple’s reliance on and operations in China (supply chain, tariffs, rare earths, and security/espionage concerns). A vote on director re-election will include chair Art Levinson and director Ron Sugar, with the board explicitly waiving its usual age-75 guideline for those two.
  • 9:00 a.m. The S&P/Case-Shiller House Price Index will be released.
  • 10:00 a.m. The Conference Board will release its latest consumer confidence report.

 

Wednesday - February 25

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Nvidia (NVDA), Salesforce (CRM), Lowe's (LOW), TJX Companies (TJX), and Snowflake (SNOW). Options trading implies a double-digit swing in share price for Redwire (RDW) and c3.ai (AI) after they report.
  • All day - Samsung's (SSNLF) Galaxy Unpacked media event will include the unveiling of the new Galaxy S26 phone lineup, showcasing of new Galaxy AI features, and potentially new Galaxy Buds.
  • All day - Lumen Technologies (LUMN) will hold its Investor Day. Management will lay out the next phase of the company's transformation, including product pipeline, commercial strategy, financial framework, and multiyear growth and earnings plans.
  • All day - Intapp (INTA) will host an Investor Day in New York, where management is expected to highlight its AI‑driven vertical SaaS platform, role in legal/advisory/capital markets workflows, and key growth and financial metrics for the business.
  • 8:00 a.m. Nasdaq (NDAQ) will hold its 2026 Investor Day at its Times Square headquarters (also webcast) from 8:00 a.m. to 12:30 p.m. ET, focusing on strategy, business mix, and long‑term financial objectives for the group.
  • 8:30 a.m. It is the FDA action date for Eton Pharmaceuticals' (ETON) ET‑600 desmopressin oral solution, which is being reviewed as a treatment for pediatric central diabetes insipidus/arginine vasopressin deficiency.
  • 10:00 a.m. The New Home Sales report will be released by the Census Bureau.
  • 5:00 p.m. Nvidia (NVDA) will hold its earnings conference call. Analysts expect crucial color on 2026–2027 AI capex plans from hyperscalers Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet (GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN) to impact the tech sector broadly. Stocks that have been jolted in the past during Nvidia's update include AMD (AMD), Intel (INTC), Super Micro Computer (SMCI), and Broadcom (AVGO). Options trading implies a 6% share price move for Nvidia after its earnings report drops.

 

Thursday - February 26

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Royal Bank of Canada (RY), Toronto-Dominion Bank (TD), Monster Beverage (MNST), Intuit (INTU), Dell Technologies (DELL), and Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). Options trading implies double-digit swings for Duolingo (DUOL) and Figs (FIGS) after they report.
  • All day - Fed Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman is scheduled for Senate Banking Committee testimony.
  • 8:30 a.m. The January Durable Goods orders report will be released.

 

Friday - February 27

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Pearson (PSO).
  • 8:30 a.m. The January Producer Price Index report will be released by the Bureau of Labor Statistics.
  • 9:00 a.m. The U.S. Census Bureau will release the January Construction Spending report.
  • All day - Jack in the Box (JACK) will hold its annual shareholder meeting and director election, where the company is urging investors to back its full slate of 10 board nominees amid activist investor pushback.

data.title: Casella signals 8% revenue growth and $455M–$465M EBITDA guidance for 2026 while advancing Mid-Atlantic integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Casella Waste Systems (CWST) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Ned Coletta, President & CEO, opened his first call as CEO by emphasizing the company's fifth consecutive year of double-digit growth in revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted free cash flow. He noted, "For the full year 2025, revenues increased 18%, Adjusted EBITDA increased 17%, and adjusted free cash flow increased 14%." Coletta highlighted sustained organic growth, operating improvements, and strategic momentum, with a particular focus on margin expansion and disciplined collection pricing. He confirmed the completion of nine acquisitions in 2025 representing over $115 million in annualized revenues, and the January 2026 closing of the Mountain State Waste acquisition, which adds $30 million in annualized revenues and expands operations into West Virginia.

  • Coletta also detailed key operational initiatives, including efficiencies from new automated trucks and significant progress on system integration in the Mid-Atlantic segment. He pointed to expansion efforts at the Hakes and Hyland landfills, expecting the Hakes permit "in the next couple of quarters and the Hyland permit expected within the year." Coletta stated, "We are working to more than double the annual permit at Hyland from 460,000 tons a year to 1,000,000 tons, and we would also add close to 60 years of capacity at current run rate."

  • CFO Bradford Helgeson reported, "Revenues in the fourth quarter were $469.1 million, up $41.6 million, or 9.7% year-over-year, with $23.1 million from acquisitions... and $18.5 million from same-store growth, or 4.3%." Helgeson highlighted solid waste revenue growth, margin expansion, and the impact of risk-sharing structures offsetting commodity volatility in the recycling segment.

Outlook

  • Management announced 2026 financial guidance of revenue between $1.97 billion and $1.99 billion, representing 8% growth at the midpoint. Adjusted EBITDA is projected between $455 million and $465 million, or 9% growth at the midpoint, and adjusted free cash flow is forecasted in the $195 million to $205 million range. Helgeson explained, "Our guidance ranges reflect acquisitions completed to date, including Mountain State Waste, which closed on January 1, and assume a stable economic environment for the balance of the year."

  • The guidance includes approximately $60 million from acquisitions, about 4.5% organic growth, and plans for 5% pricing in the solid waste business. Management expects "solid waste volumes are expected to be approximately flat plus or minus, with continued churn in our collection book of business reflected in that estimate."

  • Helgeson stated, "Our adjusted EBITDA guidance range implies approximately flat margins to 40 basis points of margin improvement in 2026, which is largely the base business."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue was reported at $469.1 million. Adjusted EBITDA in the quarter was $107 million, up $12 million or 12.7% year-over-year, with a margin of 22.8%, up about 60 basis points.

  • Adjusted net income was $18.9 million, or $0.30 per diluted share. Net cash provided by operating activities for the year was $329.8 million, and adjusted free cash flow was $179.9 million. Capital expenditures totaled $245.1 million, including $66 million related to acquisitions. Total debt as of December 31 was $1.17 billion, with $124 million in cash and a consolidated net leverage ratio of 2.34x.

Q&A

  • Patrick Brown, Raymond James: Asked about Coletta's long-term vision for Casella and the pace of M&A. Coletta responded there will be "no major right turn," reiterating a continued focus on existing strategies: "We're focused on the same building blocks that have created a lot of value for shareholders over many years." Brown also inquired about synergies in the Mid-Atlantic; Coletta clarified $5 million in expected savings for 2026, noting operational, routing, and pricing opportunities unfolding beyond 2026.

  • Tami Zakaria, JPMorgan: Asked about volume growth. Coletta and Helgeson emphasized internalization and indicated landfill volumes should be a positive for growth in 2026, with collection volumes expected to be flat. Zakaria followed up on G&A improvement; Helgeson pointed to ongoing technology and process upgrades, aiming to lower G&A as a percentage of revenue in future years.

  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about the economics of internalizing landfill volumes. Coletta described recent efforts to prioritize internalization for stability and value, stating, "We got a lot of that work done... it gives more stability to the business." Bubes asked about Ontario landfill closure planning post-2028, with Coletta and Helgeson indicating efforts to migrate tons and improve cash flow and earnings profile without major EBITDA impact.

  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: Asked about 2026 M&A pipeline and scale. Coletta reported the pipeline is "really good in the advanced stage," with potential to surpass $150 million in acquired revenue for 2026. Romeo also questioned the impact of facility closures; Coletta confirmed headwinds were factored into guidance.

  • James Schumm, TD Cowen: Queried the breakdown of $5 million in Mid-Atlantic automation synergies. Helgeson explained it includes labor savings and route consolidation, with further G&A efficiencies expected in 2027 as redundant systems are phased out.

  • Stephanie Benjamin Moore, Jefferies: Asked about Mid-Atlantic pricing opportunity. Helgeson and Coletta noted the region saw about 3% pricing versus 5% elsewhere, with future improvement expected after integration.

  • Shlomo Rosenbaum, Stifel: Sought details on national accounts growth and weather-related headwinds. Coletta highlighted strong industrial business growth and described significant winter weather impacts, though the company maintained its outlook.

  • William Grippin, Barclays: Asked about the outlook for landfill pricing and future price movement. Coletta explained the market has seen some capacity shifts but is now "back to the point for the first time... where quality of revenue, driving returns is a big, big focus."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was largely constructive but probing, with repeated focus on Mid-Atlantic integration, volume growth, and pricing opportunities. Questions often sought clarity on timelines and quantification of synergy capture, with some skepticism on realizing operational improvements within guidance periods.

  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident, with Coletta expressing energy for growth and Helgeson emphasizing operational and financial discipline. In Q&A, management maintained a steady, slightly cautious tone, often describing guidance as "conservative" and deferring specifics beyond current planning cycles. Trigger phrases included "we're being a touch conservative" and "there's more to come here."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone was somewhat more insistent on follow-up details around integration and cost reduction, while management remained consistent in projecting stable execution and incremental improvement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 maintained the preliminary outlook provided in Q3, with revenue growth of 8% and adjusted EBITDA growth of 9% at the midpoint. The shift in CEO leadership from John Casella to Ned Coletta was completed, but strategic priorities remained unchanged, with a continued focus on organic growth, disciplined M&A, and Mid-Atlantic integration.

  • Analysts in both quarters concentrated on margin expansion, acquisition cadence, and Mid-Atlantic operational improvements. This quarter, there was increased discussion of G&A leverage starting in 2027 and specific headwinds from facility closures, which were referenced as manageable within updated guidance.

  • Key metrics showed a decrease in Q4 revenue and adjusted EBITDA compared to Q3, reflecting seasonality and acquisition timing, both directly addressed by management.

  • Management's tone remained confident, emphasizing disciplined growth and conservative guidance, while analysts pressed more on the specifics of synergy realization and cost controls.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted operational headwinds from severe winter weather but expressed confidence in the team's performance and the resilience of the business model.

  • Facility closures, such as Hawk Ridge Organics and the upcoming Ontario County landfill closure in 2028, were discussed as headwinds. Management described mitigation plans, including landfill permit expansions and internalization strategies to maintain returns and avoid major EBITDA impact.

  • Analysts highlighted the need for successful integration of recent acquisitions and the associated systems migration, with concern for timing and the realization of expected synergies and cost reductions.

Final Takeaway

Casella Waste Systems delivered another year of double-digit growth in revenue, EBITDA, and free cash flow, supported by disciplined pricing, operational improvements, and strategic acquisitions. The company enters 2026 with robust guidance, a strong acquisition pipeline, and renewed focus on system integration and automation, especially in the Mid-Atlantic region. Management remains confident in delivering incremental margin expansion, navigating facility closures, and leveraging technology investments to drive efficient, scalable growth while maintaining a conservative approach to guidance and capital allocation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Strawberry Fields targets $100M–$150M annual growth with 13% AFFO CAGR amid disciplined acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Strawberry Fields REIT, Inc. (STRW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jeffrey Bajtner, COO & Chief Investment Officer, opened the call by highlighting that "throughout 2025, the company collected 100% of its contractual rents," emphasizing the strength of their disciplined investment approach. He noted the retenanted 10 Kentucky properties with Hill Valley as the new tenant at a base rent of $23.3 million per year, subject to 2.5% annual increases, and the acquisition of 6 Kansas facilities for $24 million. Bajtner stated, “in June, the company issued ILS 312 million in Series B Bonds on the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange, which is approximately $89.5 million. The bonds are unsecured and were issued at par with a fixed interest rate of 6.7%.” He also cited the $59 million acquisition of 9 Missouri facilities and a dividend increase from $0.14 to $0.16 per share, a 14% rise. Bajtner concluded: “2025 was the best year the company has had since its inception. Over the last 5 years, the company has had 13-plus percent growth of the adjusted FFO, adjusted EBITDA and the average base rents.”
  • Greg Flamion, Chief Financial Officer, stated: “Total assets are $885 million, an increase of $97.9 million or 12.4% compared to December 31, 2024.” He reported “2025 revenue was $155 million, up $37.9 million compared to December 31, 2024,” and a “year-to-date net income of $33.3 million or $0.60 per share compared to $26.5 million or $0.57 per share in 2024.” Flamion highlighted “2025 AFFO is $72.5 million. This is a growth of 29.8% versus 2024 and represents a 13.3% compound annual growth rate.”
  • CEO Moishe Gubin commented: “proudly from $38 million to $72 million. These are really good numbers that we’re very proud of.” He emphasized, “our stock price, which in December, we hit an all-time high. We've got to $14 a share. And we're still way undervalued.” Gubin reiterated their strategy as a “pure-play skilled nursing facility, healthcare REIT,” and targeted “guidance of being able to grow $100 million to $150 million a year.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated their growth target, with Gubin stating, “we’re still anticipating guidance of being able to grow $100 million to $150 million a year. We hope to beat that.” He also noted plans to keep “staying above 90% in skilled nursing facilities.”
  • Gubin projected, “we ended up the year with an AFFO of $1.30. We should beat that easily in '26.”

Financial Results

  • Flamion reported, “2025 revenue was $155 million, up $37.9 million compared to December 31, 2024.” Net income was $33.3 million or $0.60 per share. AFFO reached $72.5 million, a 29.8% increase versus 2024. Adjusted EBITDA was $125.3 million, a 38.2% increase compared to 2024. The dividend was increased to $0.16 a share, with an AFFO payout of 46%.
  • Bajtner detailed the portfolio: “143 facilities located in 10 states, which comprises 15,602 licensed beds. The total value of our portfolio at acquisition is $1.1 billion. Our acquisition pipeline remains strong at $250 million.”
  • Gubin noted, “our AFFO payout ratio continues to be the lowest, where we’re paying out of 47% or close to 47% of our AFFO using the rest of the money to pay down debt as a placeholder, but to be able to use it to buy more assets.”

Q&A

  • Richard Anderson, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about EBITDARM coverage and free cash flow after dividends. Gubin responded the number is “right around $40 million after everything said and done, that we are stockpiling.” Anderson inquired about Medicare Advantage exposure; Gubin explained, “we don’t have any of our rents that are predicated on results of our tenants and our rent changing up or down as bonus rent or not bonus rent. So we don’t have -- we don’t suffer from that at all.”
  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners: Inquired about 2026 debt maturities and new rates. Gubin said, “the line of credit debt is going to come back in at SOFR 2.70 about. SOFR 2.65, 2.75 right around there and that the bond debt is going to come in around 6.25%.” On G&A, Flamion noted a “onetime item. We had some additional payroll that came through in Q4 due to additional executive compensation.” Gubin clarified, “the onetime event is I finally got a raise… they recorded about $1 million or $1.1 million in a onetime thing.”
  • Mark Smith, Lake Street: Asked about acquisition pipeline and occupancy. Gubin emphasized, “we’ve never had an impediment as far as cash,” while Bajtner noted, “the deals are coming in day in, day out.” On occupancy, Gubin said, “our revenue is not based on… occupancy… we’re showing 100% occupied because every building that we have has been leased out and we get paid a rent no matter how full they are.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of payout ratio, dividend growth, debt refinancing, and acquisition pipeline, with a generally positive tone. There was curiosity about risks but no signs of significant skepticism.
  • Management was confident throughout, frequently highlighting disciplined investment and growth. Gubin used language such as, “we are confident and we know that debt markets… are open,” and “we feel like we’re going to just keep sticking with our guns.”
  • Compared to last quarter, both analysts and management maintained a slightly more upbeat tone, with management emphasizing record results and growth targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remains consistent, with management reiterating a $100 million to $150 million annual growth target. The AFFO compound annual growth rate was again highlighted at over 13%. Dividend increased to $0.16 per share from $0.14, and AFFO payout ratio remains below 50%.
  • Strategic focus remains on acquisitions, maintaining a pure-play skilled nursing portfolio, and strengthening the balance sheet. Management’s language around portfolio quality and undervaluation of the stock continues from the previous quarter.
  • Analysts’ questions continue to focus on dividend policy, debt structure, and acquisition pipeline, with an ongoing interest in how the company will fund future growth. Management’s confidence in their disciplined approach and ability to close deals appeared stronger this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Gubin addressed risks related to Medicare Advantage, stating there is “no exposure” to bonus rent fluctuations. He also discussed potential interest rate changes on debt refinancing but modeled new debt at reasonable rates and expects maturity extension.
  • On G&A, one-time executive compensation was noted as a non-recurring item. Management does not expect further G&A increases in 2026.
  • Analysts questioned occupancy levels, but management reiterated rent collection is not tied to occupancy and emphasized a resilient revenue model.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized record growth in revenue, AFFO, and portfolio size in 2025, highlighted by full rent collection, a disciplined acquisition strategy, and a robust acquisition pipeline. The company reaffirmed its annual growth target of $100 million to $150 million, maintained a low payout ratio, and reported increasing dividends. With a focus on maintaining a pure-play skilled nursing portfolio and managing risk through stable master leases, management expressed strong confidence in ongoing expansion and the ability to sustain and increase shareholder returns in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DNOW outlines $23M year-one synergy target and delays guidance amid ERP integration challenges
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: DNOW Inc. (DNOW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Cherechinsky opened by highlighting the completed merger with MRC Global, stating, "We are now together, building our strengths, talent and collective ambitions under one roof... Without a doubt, we are better together." He emphasized the record performance of legacy DNOW, noting, "In 2025, legacy DNOW achieved a record full year EBITDA of $199 million, establishing a new annual record for EBITDA results." Cherechinsky also announced, "We are on track to achieve year 1 cost synergies faster than planned and now expect to reach $23 million in cost savings by the end of the first year compared to the $17 million we said we would achieve for 2026." He acknowledged significant ERP system challenges inherited from MRC Global, affecting around 40% of DNOW's business, with the team "mobilized to execute a comprehensive remediation plan to capture and resolve the most critical obstacles."

  • Cherechinsky described the merger as a catalyst for "meaningful expansion and diversification of our business across 4 core sectors," and highlighted new market penetration, including supplying to 11 new data center customers within a year of entry. He also noted the pursuit of cross-selling and revenue synergies, leveraging the combined footprint and product portfolio.

  • CFO Mark Johnson reported, "Revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $959 million, up 51% or $325 million from the third quarter of 2025, driven by $388 million of MRC Global contribution from the close date of November 6 through the year-end 2025." He explained the impact of acquisition-related costs and noted, "Our balance sheet remains healthy with ample liquidity of $588 million, including $424 million in availability on our credit facility and $164 million of cash at the end of the fourth quarter."

Outlook

  • Management announced a delay in issuing sequential and full-year guidance, citing "persistent challenges related to our ERP implementation within legacy MRC Global U.S. operations, while we are simultaneously operating in a critical phase of integration." Cherechinsky stated, "We will reinstate guidance when we have greater operational stability and predictability in MRC Global U.S. operations once we have more clarity." He added, "Generally, we see our overall business with kind of a flattish revenue. That's how I saw going into 2026, flattish revenue, very little revenue change organically."

  • Sector commentary included expectations for upstream to be "flat to down," midstream to see growth, gas utilities to present opportunities as ERP issues are resolved, and downstream to benefit from an active maintenance season.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue reached $959 million, supported by the MRC Global merger. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $61 million, while full-year EBITDA totaled $209 million. Adjusted net income for the quarter was $23 million, or $0.15 per fully diluted share. Johnson reported that U.S. revenue for the quarter was $765 million, with legacy DNOW revenue down approximately 10% sequentially due to seasonality and ERP impacts.

  • The company incurred $135 million of acquisition-related costs and $50 million of transaction-related expenses in the quarter. The fourth quarter net loss was $147 million, "unfavorably impacted by the fourth quarter merger-related costs, including approximately $50 million of transaction-related costs, $12 million in DTA reclassification charges and $135 million in inventory step-up to fair value amortization charges related to the MRC Global merger." Working capital as a percentage of annualized fourth quarter revenue rose to 29.7% due to partial period impact from MRC Global.

Q&A

  • Adam Farley, Stifel: Asked about the impact of MRC's ERP transition and timeline for resolution. Cherechinsky explained, "the ERP issues are limited to U.S. MRC only... revenue loss attributable fully to the ERP implementation, both in the third and fourth quarters... We have all hands on deck... We're not really sure when the resolution happens, but here's what we're doing to mitigate in the short term." Farley also inquired about 2026 growth expectations and cost synergy targets, with Cherechinsky reiterating flattish revenue and focus on achieving $70 million in cost synergies over three years.

  • Alexander Rygiel, Texas Capital: Sought strategic vision for revenue growth and margins. Cherechinsky responded, "Generally, we see our overall business with kind of a flattish revenue... the real benefits from DNOW and MRC coming together are these things... better buying, better product availability, standardization, customers clamoring for access to products." Rygiel also asked about retaining key employees amid ERP headwinds, with Cherechinsky outlining efforts to reward and incentivize top talent.

  • Charles Minervino, Susquehanna: Probed if ERP issues had peaked. Cherechinsky said, "things are better... We're coping. We are cleaning up old problems. We're fast-tracking solutions... I'll have a better feel for it in the next 80 days or so." On free cash flow, Cherechinsky expressed confidence in generating strong cash flow as inventory and receivables normalize.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly negative, pressing management for clarity on ERP challenges, growth expectations, and the path to synergy realization, reflecting concern about operational disruptions and delayed guidance.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, especially regarding merger benefits and cost synergy progress, but appeared more cautious and defensive during Q&A, frequently acknowledging uncertainty and emphasizing ongoing efforts to resolve issues. Cherechinsky used phrases like "we're not really sure when the resolution happens" and "we are coping," signaling a tempered confidence compared to the previous quarter.

  • Compared to Q3, sentiment shifted from steady confidence and growth focus to a more cautious stance, with increased analyst skepticism and management defensiveness due to ERP disruptions and the decision to suspend guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter marks a major shift with the completed MRC Global merger, bringing substantial revenue growth and new integration challenges, especially around the ERP system—a contrast to Q3 where the merger was anticipated but not yet closed.

  • Guidance language changed from providing specific EBITDA and free cash flow targets to delaying guidance amid operational uncertainty. Previously, management forecasted full-year EBITDA approaching 8% of revenues and free cash flow near $150 million; currently, no such targets are offered.

  • Analysts' attention moved from synergy opportunities and growth prospects to operational risks and the timing of ERP issue resolution. Management's tone shifted from optimistic to more guarded and defensive.

  • Key metrics such as revenue, EBITDA, and costs saw significant increases due to the merger, but also brought higher transaction and integration-related expenses and a net loss for the quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • The most pressing risk is the persistent ERP implementation challenges within U.S. MRC Global operations, described as "resulting in inefficiencies for certain core processes, continuing negative operating and financial impacts." Management is executing a remediation plan and fast-tracking migration of upstream locations to DNOW's SAP system.

  • Acquisition-related costs, higher working capital, and inventory step-up charges impacted profitability and cash flow.

  • Analysts raised concerns about the duration and breadth of ERP challenges, potential for further operational disruption, and employee retention amid integration.

Final Takeaway

The company emphasized that while the merger with MRC Global has significantly scaled the business and unlocked new sector opportunities, near-term results are dominated by integration and ERP system challenges that are weighing on financial performance and delaying the return of guidance. Management remains focused on resolving these obstacles, capturing cost synergies ahead of plan, and leveraging the expanded footprint for future growth, with a commitment to reinstate guidance once operational stability is achieved.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold reclaims $5,000 and silver pops after the Supreme Court tariff ruling
data.description:
Gold and silver rallied sharply Friday afternoon as investors reacted to a significant legal development in Washington, driving renewed momentum across the precious metals complex.

Spot gold (XAUUSD:CUR) climbed 1.5% on the session to trade near $5,073 per ounce, pushing decisively back above the $5,000 threshold. Meanwhile, spot silver (XAGUSD:CUR) jumped 5.7% to $82.90 per ounce, marking its strongest level in roughly a week. 

The advance followed a closely watched 6-3 ruling from the U.S. Supreme Court that struck down former President Donald Trump’s tariffs. The decision invalidated the administration’s use of emergency powers to implement sweeping trade levies without explicit congressional authorization.

Market participants appeared to interpret the ruling as a catalyst for volatility across currency and macro-sensitive assets, prompting renewed demand for hard assets. Gold, often viewed as a hedge against policy uncertainty and geopolitical risk, attracted steady inflows.

Silver outperformed on a percentage basis amid heightened speculative interest, underscoring how quickly legal and policy developments can ripple through global commodity markets.

Gold ETFs: (GLD), (IAU), (SGOL), (OUNZ), (BAR), (GDX), (GDXJ), (NUGT), (RING), and (DUST). 

Silver ETFs: (SLV), (SIVR), (AGQ), (ZSL), (PSLV), (SIL), (SILJ), and (SLVP).

data.title: Southern Company wins two analyst upgrades after guidance 'clearly ahead' of expectations
data.description:
Southern Company (SO) won two analyst upgrades Friday, a day after the Atlanta utility reported Q4 results and raised its long-term earnings per share compound annual growth rate to 7%-8% from 5%-7% previously while raising its five-year spending plan by ~7%, reflecting the surging demand for power in the U.S.

The company, which said it had contracted 10 GW of large-load customers across Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi, now plans to spend ~$81B through 2030, compared with its prior five-year plan of $76B, with about half of the spending directed to boosting power generation.

Data centers with a combined demand of ~75 GW have expressed interest in connecting to Southern's (SO) system, executives said on the company's earnings conference call.

Southern (SO) was upgraded at Mizuho to Outperform from Neutral with a $104 price target, raised from $89, saying the company still presents an attractive valuation for a fully regulated story "growing at 8% and possibly more," even after the stock's run-up.

"While investors will continue to focus on the political noise, we believe growth will drown out this noise and remind investors that the next GP rate case is in 2028," Mizuho's Anthony Crowdell wrote, adding that the shares have historically traded at one of the higher P/Es in the sector, "and with this quarterly update, we expect the march toward the mid-double-digit premium will begin."

Relative to other 8%-plus growers, the analyst likes that Southern (SO) is 90% fully regulated, does not depend on one customer, operates in a vertically integrated world, and has a Baa1 balance sheet.

The stock also was raised at Wells Fargo to Equal Weight from Underweight with a $96 PT, up from $84, saying updated guidance was "clearly ahead of our expectations, which came as a surprise and despite the affordability backdrop/optics" in Georgia.

Wells Fargo analyst Shahriar Pourreza said he has always believed in Southern's (SO) management team and core fundamentals, and his main concerns were centered around the political backdrop and affordability optics weighing on valuation, a risk that was "clearly overestimated."

Southern (SO) shares -0.7% on Friday after jumping 4.5% in the previous session following earnings.
